title: IELTS Grammar Roadmap — Band 7+
subtitle: 42-day advanced grammar mastery for IELTS Writing & Speaking — 1 hr/day

weeks.title: Week 1 — Tense & Aspect Mastery
weeks.goal: Band 7+ examiners look for a wide range of structures used accurately. This week: stop using only Present Simple + Past Simple. Master perfect aspect, continuous nuances, future variety, and narrative tense flow.

days.title: The 12 Tenses — Band 7 Precision Audit

blocks.content: Map all 12 tenses on one page: 4 aspects (Simple, Continuous, Perfect, Perfect Continuous) × 3 times (Past, Present, Future). For each, write the form (auxiliary + main verb) and one IELTS-appropriate example. Most Band 6 candidates only use 4–5 tenses fluently — your goal is at least 8.

Audit your own writing samples for: (1) using Past Simple when Present Perfect is needed (I have lived here for 5 years, not I lived here for 5 years), (2) overusing Present Continuous for habitual actions, (3) mixing tenses within the same sentence without reason.

Pick 5 IELTS Writing Task 2 topics. For each, write one sentence per tense (12 sentences). Check that each sentence is natural — Band 7+ is not about cramming all tenses, but about choosing the right one for the meaning.

In Speaking Part 2, candidates often slip back to Past Simple under pressure. Record yourself describing a memory using Past Simple + Past Continuous + Past Perfect in the same answer (I had been waiting for an hour when she finally arrived).

days.deliverable: One-page tense map + 12 self-written IELTS-context sentences (one per tense)

days.title: Present Perfect vs Past Simple — IELTS-critical contrast

blocks.content: The single highest-yield tense distinction in IELTS. Past Simple = finished time (yesterday, in 2010, last year). Present Perfect = unfinished time / result-now (since, for, recently, already, yet, ever). Native examiners notice this immediately.

Technology has changed dramatically over the last decade (effect continues now → Present Perfect). Technology changed dramatically in the 2000s (finished period → Past Simple). Both grammatically correct — but only one fits the meaning.

Write 10 sentences for Writing Task 1 (line graph / bar chart) using Present Perfect when the trend reaches the present, and Past Simple when the data ends in the past. This is the #1 grammar issue in Task 1.
Open any Writing Task 2 introduction you've written. Replace 2–3 Past Simples with Present Perfects where the result still applies today. Notice how the writing instantly sounds more sophisticated.

days.deliverable: Side-by-side rewrite of one Task 1 + one Task 2 paragraph applying the distinction

days.title: Past Perfect & Past Perfect Continuous in narratives

blocks.content: Past Perfect (had + V3) = an action completed before another past action. Past Perfect Continuous (had been + V-ing) = an action that continued up to a point in the past. These two tenses are what separate Band 6 storytelling from Band 7+.

By the time I reached the airport, my flight had already taken off. / She had been studying for 3 hours when the power went out. Each one collapses two sentences into one and shows time-relationship clearly.

Take 5 simple two-sentence stories (I went to the shop. The shop closed.) and rewrite each as one sentence using Past Perfect or Past Perfect Continuous. Vary the connectors: by the time, when, before, after, until.
Speaking Part 2 cue cards often ask for past experiences. Plan a 2-minute answer about a difficult journey using at least 3 past tenses (Simple, Continuous, Perfect). Record and check.

days.deliverable: Recorded 2-minute Speaking Part 2 answer using all 4 past tenses

days.title: Future Forms — 6 ways to talk about the future

blocks.content: Band 7+ writers don't only use will. The 6 future forms: (1) will (prediction/decision), (2) be going to (intention/evidence-based prediction), (3) Present Continuous (arrangement), (4) Present Simple (timetable), (5) Future Continuous (action in progress at a future point), (6) Future Perfect (completed by a future point).

By 2050, automation will have replaced many manual jobs (Future Perfect — completion). This time next year, I'll be living in Sydney (Future Continuous — ongoing). Governments are launching new climate policies in March (Present Continuous — fixed plan).
Write a 150-word prediction paragraph about cities in 2050 using all 6 future forms at least once. Highlight each form in a different colour to confirm range.
Future Perfect is rare and high-value: in Task 2 conclusions about the future, drop in one sentence with will have + V3. It signals advanced control.

days.deliverable: 150-word future-prediction paragraph using all 6 future forms

days.title: Stative vs Dynamic Verbs (the “I am loving it” trap)

blocks.content: Stative verbs describe states, not actions, and normally do not take the continuous form: know, believe, understand, own, belong, contain, prefer, love, hate, mean, seem, exist. Saying I am knowing or I am owning is a dead giveaway of Band 5–6.

❌ I am understanding the question. ✔ I understand the question. Some verbs are dual-use with different meanings: I think you're right (opinion — stative) vs I'm thinking about the problem (mental activity — dynamic).

Make a personal list of 15 stative verbs you use most often. For each, write one sentence in Simple form. Then identify any verbs that can be dynamic with a different meaning (have, see, taste, smell, think, feel).
Watch out in Speaking — under stress, candidates say I am thinking it is important. Train yourself to default to I think, I believe, I find.

days.deliverable: Personal list of 15 stative verbs + dual-use chart for 6 verbs

days.title: Sequence of Tenses & Backshift in Reported Speech

blocks.content: When the reporting verb is past (said, told, claimed), tenses in the reported clause usually shift one step back: Present → Past, Present Perfect → Past Perfect, will → would, can → could, may → might. Place/time references shift too: here → there, now → then, today → that day, tomorrow → the next day.

Direct: “I have finished my homework,” she said. → Reported: She said (that) she had finished her homework. Notice the clause connector that is optional in informal use, but Band 7+ writing usually keeps it.
No backshift when the reported statement is still true / a general truth / a future-still-pending: The teacher said the Earth orbits the Sun. Examiners reward writers who recognise these subtleties.
Convert 10 direct-speech sentences (mix of Present, Present Perfect, will, can) into reported speech. Then write 3 sentences where backshift is optional and explain why.

days.deliverable: Reported-speech conversion sheet (10 + 3 exceptions explained)

days.title: Week 1 Review — Tense Variety in Writing Task 2

blocks.content: Self-test without notes: name and form all 12 tenses. List 5 stative verbs. Explain when no backshift is needed in reported speech. Explain the difference between I have lived here for 5 years and I lived here for 5 years.

Write a full Writing Task 2 essay (250+ words) on: Some people believe that technology has made our lives easier; others argue it has created new problems. Discuss both views and give your opinion. Aim for at least 8 different tenses across the essay.

Highlight every verb in your essay. Annotate the tense of each. If you find yourself stuck in Present Simple for 5+ verbs in a row, rewrite at least 2 to use Present Perfect, Future Perfect, or a participle clause.

days.deliverable: 250-word Task 2 essay with verb-by-verb tense annotation

weeks.title: Week 2 — Conditionals & Hypothetical Forms
weeks.goal: Conditionals are gold for Band 7+. They show complex thinking: cause-effect, hypothesis, regret, advice. Mixed and inverted forms in particular signal control well above Band 6.

days.title: Zero, First, Second, Third — precision recap

blocks.content: Zero: If + Present, Present — general truths (If water reaches 100°C, it boils). First: If + Present, will + V — likely future. Second: If + Past, would + V — unlikely/imaginary present. Third: If + Past Perfect, would have + V3 — impossible past.
❌ If I would have time, I would help you. ✔ If I had time, I would help you. Never use would in the if-clause in standard conditionals (rare exceptions with would for politeness).

Write 4 conditional sentences on a Band-7 topic (climate change, education, technology) — one of each type. Then rewrite each, swapping if for unless / provided that / as long as where it fits naturally.
Speaking Part 3 questions like What would happen if everyone used public transport? demand Second Conditional. Practice 5 hypothetical Part-3 answers, each starting with If…, …would…

days.deliverable: 4-type conditional set + 5 hypothetical Speaking Part 3 answers

days.title: Mixed Conditionals — the Band 7+ signature

blocks.content: Mixed conditionals combine times. Type A — past condition, present result: If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now (didn't study → not a doctor today). Type B — present condition, past result: If I were better organised, I wouldn't have missed the deadline (general trait → past consequence).
Mixed conditionals are rare even among Band 7 candidates. Using just one accurate mixed conditional in a Task 2 essay or Speaking Part 3 answer is a strong Band 7.5–8 signal.

Write 6 mixed conditional sentences — 3 of Type A, 3 of Type B — on real personal or social topics (career, education, environment). Test each by checking that the time of the if-clause and time of the result clause are clearly different.
Memorise one fluent Type-A template: If I hadn't [past action], I wouldn't be [present state] today. Use it when describing a turning point in Speaking Part 2.

days.deliverable: 6 mixed-conditional sentences (3 Type A + 3 Type B) on social topics

days.title: Inverted Conditionals (Were I to..., Had I known..., Should you...)

blocks.content: Drop if and invert subject-auxiliary. First Conditional: Should you need help, please contact me. Second: Were I to choose again, I would pick a different career. Third: Had I known the risks, I would never have invested. These are formal, written, and a Band 7.5+ marker.

In Writing Task 2 — especially in formal letter / report / discursive essays. They give a discursive register that If you should need help simply doesn't have. Don't overuse: one per essay is impressive; three is showing off and may sound forced.

Take 6 ordinary conditional sentences from your past writing and rewrite each in the inverted form. Then check: does it still sound natural in context? If not, the inversion is forced — use the original.

Find one place in your most recent Task 2 conclusion to insert Were governments to act decisively… or Should this trend continue…. That single sentence raises the perceived register of the whole conclusion.

days.deliverable: 6 conditional sentences rewritten in inverted form, judged for naturalness

days.title: Wish / If only / I'd rather / It's (high) time

blocks.content: All four use the unreal past (Past Simple or Past Perfect) even when meaning is present or future. I wish I knew the answer (don't know now). If only I had studied harder (regret about past). I'd rather you didn't smoke here (preference about now). It's high time we acted on climate change (it should be happening now).
❌ I wish I will pass. ✔ I hope I will pass. Use wish + would only for complaints about other people's behaviour: I wish he would stop interrupting.
Write 8 sentences expressing regrets or preferences — 2 each with wish, if only, I'd rather, it's (high) time. Make 4 about the present and 4 about the past.
Speaking Part 3 questions like How could your country improve education? are perfect for It's high time the government invested more in… Practice 3 such answers.

days.deliverable: 8-sentence regret/preference set + 3 high-time Speaking Part 3 answers

days.title: Alternatives to “if” — provided that, supposing, unless, as long as, in case

blocks.content: Provided that / providing = on the condition that (formal). As long as = same, more conversational. Unless = if not (used with positive verb). Supposing / suppose = imagine (hypothetical, often spoken). In case = as a precaution (different meaning — not really a conditional).

❌ Unless you don't study, you will fail. ✔ Unless you study, you will fail. Unless already contains the negative. Also: in case ≠ if — Take an umbrella in case it rains means so that you are prepared if, not if.

Rewrite 10 plain if-sentences on social/educational topics using these alternatives. Check that the meaning didn't shift (especially watch unless for double-negatives and in case for the precaution sense).
Band 7+ essays vary connectors. Drop provided that or as long as into one body paragraph in your next Task 2 to break the if-if-if repetition.

days.deliverable: 10 rewrites with unless / provided that / as long as / in case / supposing

days.title: Conditionals in Speaking Part 3 (hypothetical questions)

blocks.content: Examiners specifically use “What would happen if…”, “How would things change if…”, “Do you think it would be better if…” in Part 3 to test whether you can handle hypothetical grammar. A Band 6 candidate answers in Present Simple. A Band 7+ answers in Second/Mixed conditional.

If everyone X, society would Y. / Were people to X, the result would be Y. / If we hadn't done X in the past, we wouldn't be facing Y today. Memorise 3 templates so you can deploy them under pressure.

Pick 8 real Part 3 hypothetical questions (search IELTS Speaking Part 3 hypothetical questions). Record a 30-second answer for each, using a conditional form. Listen back: did you slip into Present Simple?

Don't change the conditional type mid-answer — if you start with If everyone used public transport, the air would be cleaner, don't follow with and people will save money. Stay in the would world throughout the answer.

days.deliverable: 8 recorded hypothetical Speaking Part 3 answers using 3+ conditional forms

days.title: Week 2 Review — Conditional-rich opinion paragraph

blocks.content: Without notes: write the form for each of the 4 standard conditionals + the 2 mixed types. Explain why Unless you don't try, you'll fail is wrong. Give one inverted-conditional example for each of First/Second/Third.

Write a 250-word opinion paragraph on: Some governments are considering banning private cars in city centres. Discuss the consequences. Use at least 5 different conditional forms: Zero / First / Second / Third / Mixed / Inverted.

Highlight every if / unless / provided / had / were / should in the paragraph. If you only used if, rewrite at least 2 with alternatives. The paragraph should not feel formulaic — naturalness beats variety if forced.

days.deliverable: 250-word paragraph using 5+ distinct conditional forms, naturalness-checked

weeks.title: Week 3 — Complex Sentence Structures
weeks.goal: Band 7 says uses a variety of complex structures. Band 8 says uses a wide range of structures with full flexibility and accuracy. This week is the difference: relative clauses, participles, clefts, inversion.

days.title: Relative Clauses — defining vs non-defining (punctuation rules)

blocks.content: Defining relative clauses identify which one (no commas, that allowed): The student who scored highest got the prize. Non-defining relative clauses add extra info (commas required, that NOT allowed): My brother, who lives in Canada, is a doctor.

❌ My brother, that lives in Canada,… — non-defining never uses that. ❌ Missing commas in non-defining → makes the meaning shift completely (My brother who lives in Canada implies you have other brothers; My brother, who lives in Canada, implies one brother).

Write 10 sentences mixing the two types. Half should require commas; half must not. Then write 5 sentences using whose and of which (The book, the cover of which is torn, is mine) — these are formal Band 7+ forms.

In Writing Task 1, defining relatives compress data: The country which experienced the largest growth was India. In Task 2, non-defining relatives add explanation: Public transport, which is heavily subsidised in many cities, reduces traffic congestion.

days.deliverable: 15 relative-clause sentences (10 defining/non-defining + 5 with whose/of which)

days.title: Reduced Relative Clauses

blocks.content: Drop who/which/that + be when meaning is preserved. The students who are sitting at the back → The students sitting at the back. The book which was written by Orwell → The book written by Orwell. Reduces clutter; sounds sophisticated.

Active reduction → -ing form: People who live in cities → People living in cities. Passive reduction → -ed form: Houses that are built of wood → Houses built of wood. Also reduces with be + adjective: The applicants who are eligible → The applicants eligible.
Take 12 full relative clauses from a previous essay or article and reduce as many as possible. Note which ones don't reduce well (modal verbs, certain stative verbs) — those keep the full form.
❌ People going to the gym every day they are healthy. ✔ People going to the gym every day are healthy. The reduction cannot add a redundant subject.

days.deliverable: 12 reduced-relative-clause rewrites + notes on cases where reduction failed

days.title: Participle Clauses — -ing, -ed, having + V3

blocks.content: Participle clauses replace adverbial clauses for compactness and elegance. -ing (active, simultaneous): Walking through the park, I saw a fox. -ed (passive): Built in 1850, the bridge still stands. Having + V3 (perfect, sequence): Having finished his work, he left.
Replaces While I was walking / Because the bridge was built / After he had finished — three full clauses collapsed. This is exactly what Band 8 means by full flexibility.

Dangling participles: ❌ Walking down the street, the rain started. The rain isn't walking. ✔ Walking down the street, I noticed the rain start. Always check the participle's subject is the same as the main clause subject.
Rewrite 8 two-clause sentences using participle clauses — mix all three forms (-ing, -ed, having + V3). Read each aloud to check no dangling participles.

days.deliverable: 8 participle-clause rewrites covering all three forms, dangling-checked

days.title: Cleft Sentences — It-cleft, What-cleft, All-cleft

blocks.content: Cleft = split a sentence to emphasise one part. It-cleft: It is education that drives social mobility (emphasis on education). What-cleft: What young people really need is access to mentors. All-cleft: All we need is a clear policy.
Cleft sentences are extremely under-used by IELTS candidates. Even one well-placed What governments must understand is… in a Task 2 essay opens the introduction with a Band 7.5+ feel.
Take 6 plain Subject-Verb-Object sentences and rewrite each as: an It-cleft, a What-cleft, and (where natural) an All-cleft. Compare which version sounds best in academic writing.
Don't pile clefts on top of each other. One per paragraph max. Also note tense agreement in the cleft: It was the policy that caused the problem (not that causes if the event is past).

days.deliverable: 6 sentences rewritten in 2–3 cleft forms each, best version flagged

days.title: Inversion for Emphasis (Not only..., Hardly had..., Never...)

blocks.content: Negative or restrictive adverb at the start → invert subject + auxiliary. Not only does pollution harm health, but it also damages the economy. Hardly had he arrived when the meeting started. Never have I seen such a clear example. Under no circumstances should this rule be ignored.
Triggers: Not only / Never / Rarely / Seldom / Hardly / No sooner / Little / Only then / Only when / Under no circumstances / At no point / In no way.

Rewrite 8 sentences using each trigger at least once. Check the inversion: do/does/did + S + V for simple tenses; auxiliary + S + V for others. Common error: forgetting the do/does/did for simple-tense inversions.
Not only…but also… is the safest, most natural inversion to use in Task 2 — it works in introductions, body, and conclusion. Aim to use it at least once per essay from now on.

days.deliverable: 8 inversion sentences covering all major triggers, do/does/did checked

days.title: Building Multi-Clause Sentences (subordination + coordination + relative)

blocks.content: Band 7+ sentences often have 3+ clauses: a main clause, a subordinate clause (although, because, while), and a relative or participle. Example: Although technology, which has transformed many industries, also raises privacy concerns, governments must regulate it carefully.

Don't write a 3-clause sentence by accident — plan it. (1) State the main idea. (2) Add a contrast or cause via although / because / while. (3) Embed extra info via a relative or participle clause. (4) Read aloud — if you lose breath or meaning, split it.
Build 5 three-clause sentences on different IELTS topics (education, environment, urbanisation, technology, health). Each must use: 1 subordinator + 1 relative-or-participle + 1 main clause.
Run-on sentences — over-stuffed sentences confuse the reader. Band 7+ values variety, which means a mix of short punchy sentences AND longer complex ones, not endless 4-line monsters.

days.deliverable: 5 hand-crafted 3-clause sentences, each split into short version for comparison

days.title: Week 3 Review — Rewrite simple paragraph into Band 7+ complex sentences

blocks.content: Take this Band-5 paragraph and keep it: Many people use cars. Cars cause pollution. Pollution is bad for health. Governments should act. Acting will protect people.

Rewrite the paragraph using: at least 1 relative clause, 1 participle clause, 1 cleft sentence, 1 inversion, 1 mixed conditional. Aim for 3 sentences total, not 5. Compare both versions side by side — the difference between Band 5 and Band 7+ is visible immediately.

Annotate every clause type. If you only have one type, force in another. Then rewrite ONE sentence to be deliberately short and punchy — Band 7+ writers know that short sentences are also a tool, not a sin.

days.deliverable: Side-by-side Band 5 vs Band 7+ paragraph with clause-type annotations

weeks.title: Week 4 — Voice, Modality & Subjunctive
weeks.goal: Passive voice and modal verbs are where most Band 6 candidates plateau. This week: full passive control, perfect modals for speculation, causative have/get, and the subjunctive — small details that examiners notice.

days.title: Full Passive Voice across all tenses

blocks.content: Passive = be + V3, conjugated in any tense. Present Simple: is built. Present Perfect: has been built. Past Continuous: was being built. Future Perfect: will have been built. Modal: should be built. Modal Perfect: should have been built.

Use passive when the doer is unknown / unimportant / obvious, or when you want to put the receiver in focus. Writing Task 1 (process diagrams) is heavy passive: The mixture is heated, then poured into moulds. Task 2 uses passive for objectivity: It has been argued that…

Conjugate one verb (build) into the passive across all 12 tenses. Then rewrite 10 active sentences as passive, keeping the meaning. Then rewrite 3 passives back to active where the doer matters more — that's also a Band 7+ skill.
❌ Overusing passive — Task 2 essays drowning in It is believed / It is said / It has been argued sound vague. Mix active and passive deliberately.

days.deliverable: Full passive conjugation table + 10 active→passive + 3 passive→active rewrites

days.title: Passive of Reporting Verbs (It is said that..., He is believed to be...)

blocks.content: Two structures, same meaning. Type 1: It + passive reporting verb + that-clause: It is said that smoking causes cancer. Type 2: Subject + passive reporting verb + to-infinitive: Smoking is said to cause cancer.

Common in Type 1/2: say, believe, think, claim, report, expect, know, consider, allege, estimate, assume. Tense in the to-infinitive shifts: He is believed to have stolen the money (past act); He is believed to be hiding (current act).
Both forms make Task 2 essays sound objective and academic, hedging the writer's commitment. It is widely believed that… is a far stronger opener than People believe that…
Take 8 active sentences with reporting verbs (People say…, Scientists believe…, Many think…) and rewrite each in BOTH passive forms.

days.deliverable: 8 active reports rewritten in both passive structures

days.title: Causative — have/get something done

blocks.content: Have/get + object + V3 = arrange for someone else to do something. I had my car repaired (mechanic did it, not me). She got her hair cut. Tense varies: I'm having my house painted, I'll get the document translated, I had been having my watch repaired when…
Causative also means experiencing something done to you (often unpleasant): He had his wallet stolen, She got her phone confiscated. Same form, different meaning from context.
Write 8 sentences using causative — 4 arrangements (services) and 4 experiences (negative). Mix tenses (Present Continuous, Past Simple, Future, Modal).
Speaking Part 1 has questions like Do you cut your own hair? Answering No, I usually have it cut at a salon instead of No, someone cuts my hair sounds noticeably more advanced.

days.deliverable: 8 causative sentences (4 services + 4 negative experiences) across 4 tenses

days.title: Perfect Modals — must have, should have, could have, might have

blocks.content: Modal + have + V3 = a modal meaning applied to the past. must have = strong past deduction. should/shouldn't have = past advice or regret. could have = past possibility / unrealised ability. might/may have = uncertain past possibility. can't/couldn't have = strong past negative deduction.

The lights are off — they must have left. You should have called me earlier. I could have gone to the meeting, but I didn't. She might have forgotten our appointment. He can't have finished — it only started 10 minutes ago.

Most Band 6 candidates only use modals in the present. Even one accurate must have / should have in Speaking Part 2–3 marks you as Band 7+. They're how English speakers express speculation and regret about the past.

Write 10 perfect-modal sentences mixing all 5 forms. For each, write the present-time equivalent in the modal-only form (he must be tired ↔ he must have been tired yesterday) so the contrast is clear.

days.deliverable: 10 perfect-modal sentences with present-modal counterparts

days.title: Modals for Speculation (present & past)

blocks.content: Speculation strength scale: must (95% sure positive) → can't (95% sure negative) → could / may / might (50%, neutral). Present: must be / can't be / might be. Continuous: must be working / might be working. Past: must have been / can't have been / could have been.
❌ maybe as the only speculation tool. Maybe he is tired works at Band 5. He might be tired, He could be feeling tired, He must be exhausted — that's the Band 7+ range.
Look at 5 photos (or imagine 5 scenarios) and write 4 speculative sentences about each — one strong-positive (must), one strong-negative (can't), one neutral (might/could), and one past (must have).

Part 2 cue cards often involve uncertainty (describe a person you'd like to meet). Speculative modals fit naturally: I imagine she must be very disciplined; she might also be quite reserved in interviews.

days.deliverable: 5 scenarios × 4 speculation types = 20 modal sentences

days.title: Subjunctive Mood — mandative & formulaic

blocks.content: Mandative subjunctive: after verbs/expressions of demand, suggestion, request, recommendation, the subordinate clause uses base form (no -s, no were/was distinction). I suggest that he study harder (not studies). It is essential that every student be on time (not is). The committee demands that the report be submitted by Friday.

Verbs: suggest, recommend, demand, insist, propose, request, require, advise, ask. Adjectives: essential, vital, important, crucial, necessary, imperative + that. The British alternative is should + base form (I suggest that he should study) — also accepted.
After if, wish, as if, suppose, imagine, would rather, use were for all persons in formal English: If I were you (not was). I wish I were taller. Examiners specifically reward this in Task 2.
Write 6 mandative-subjunctive sentences (academic Task 2 register: It is essential that…, We recommend that…, The government must insist that…) and 4 were-subjunctives.

days.deliverable: 10 subjunctive sentences (6 mandative + 4 were-subjunctive)

days.title: Week 4 Review — Writing Task 1 process diagram using passive

blocks.content: Without notes: conjugate the verb design in the passive across 6 tenses. Give 2 reporting-verb passives. Write 1 causative for each of the two meanings. Give 1 mandative-subjunctive sentence. Explain when to use were instead of was.

Pick any IELTS Writing Task 1 process diagram (search IELTS Task 1 process diagram). Write a 150-word description. Use passive in at least 70% of clauses. Vary the connectors: firstly, subsequently, once, after which, at this stage, finally.

Highlight every passive form. Then highlight your modals. Goal: at least 8 passives, plus 1 modal-passive (should be / must be / can be). If your description reads as a flat list, rewrite 2 sentences with reduced passive participles (Once heated, the mixture is poured…).

days.deliverable: 150-word Task 1 process description with passive coverage analysis

weeks.title: Week 5 — Cohesion, Articles & Hedging
weeks.goal: Band 7 says uses cohesion well; Band 8 says cohesion attracts no attention. This week: stop saying Firstly, Secondly, In conclusion. Master reference, hedging, nominalization, and the article cases that trip up most candidates.

days.title: Cohesive Devices — Reference & Substitution

blocks.content: Cohesion isn't only linkers. Reference: pronouns and demonstratives that point back to earlier nouns (this, that, these, those, such, the former, the latter). Substitution: replace a repeated word with one(s) / do / so / not (The red car is faster than the blue one; I think so; I hope not).
Band 6 essays repeat full noun phrases (The problem of pollution… This problem of pollution…). Band 7+ essays move to This issue / Such concerns / The latter. Examiners specifically check for this.

Take a 200-word essay you've written. Find every repeated noun. Replace half with this/that/such/the former/the latter/one. Read aloud — if any reference is unclear, restore the noun. Cohesion fails when reference is ambiguous.
❌ This is a big problem. (Standalone This with no clear referent.) ✔ This trend / This phenomenon / This concern — always pair this/that with a summarising noun in academic writing.

days.deliverable: 200-word essay revised with reference + substitution applied

days.title: Advanced Linkers for Contrast & Concession

blocks.content: Move beyond however / but. Contrast: whereas, while, on the other hand, in contrast, conversely. Concession (admitting a point against your view): although, even though, despite (the fact that), in spite of, notwithstanding, albeit, granted that, admittedly.

Despite / In spite of + noun or -ing: Despite the rain, … / Despite being tired, … Although / Even though + full clause: Although it was raining, … Mixing these is the most common Band 6 grammar error.

Write 10 contrast/concession sentences across these connectors: 2 with whereas, 2 with despite/in spite of + noun or -ing, 2 with although/even though + clause, 2 with albeit / notwithstanding, 2 with admittedly / granted that.
❌ Despite he was tired, he kept working. ✔ Despite being tired / Although he was tired. Also: don't pair although with but in the same sentence (Although it was raining, but…) — pick one.

days.deliverable: 10-sentence contrast/concession set covering all 6+ connectors

days.title: Advanced Linkers for Cause/Effect/Result

blocks.content: Move beyond so, because, therefore. Cause: because, since, as, owing to, due to, on account of, given that, in light of, given the fact that. Effect/Result: therefore, thus, hence, consequently, as a result, accordingly, with the result that.

Due to / Owing to / On account of + noun: Due to heavy rain, … Because / Since / As + full clause: Because it rained heavily, … Most candidates use due to as a preposition (The flight was cancelled due to fog) — that's correct. Errors come when due to is used to start a sentence followed by a clause: ❌ Due to it rained.
Take 8 simple because/so sentences and rewrite each twice — once with a cause linker and once with an effect linker. Vary noun-form vs clause-form connectors.

Given that / In light of are excellent for Task 2 introductions. Given that climate change is accelerating, governments must act decisively sounds far stronger than Because climate change is accelerating, governments must act.

days.deliverable: 16 cause-effect rewrites + 1 Task 2 introduction using given that

days.title: Hedging Language — academic caution

blocks.content: Academic writing avoids absolute claims. Hedging = softening the certainty of a statement. Tools: modal verbs (may, might, could, can), lexical verbs (tend to, appear to, seem to, suggest, indicate), adverbs (generally, often, typically, in many cases, to some extent), adjectives (likely, possible, probable, apparent).
Band 7+ Task 2 avoids This always happens / Everyone agrees that…. Examiners reward measured claims: This often happens / It is widely accepted that… Hedging shows critical thinking, not weakness.
Take 8 absolutist sentences from a draft (All teenagers are addicted to phones) and hedge each with at least 2 different tools. Compare.

Don't double-hedge: ❌ It might possibly tend to perhaps suggest… Pick one or two hedges per claim. Also: hedging Task 2 opinion sentences too much makes your stance unclear (I might possibly agree to some extent) — be firm in your opinion, hedge in your evidence.

days.deliverable: 8 absolutist→hedged sentence rewrites with 2+ hedge types each

days.title: Nominalization — turning verbs into noun phrases

blocks.content: Academic writing prefers noun phrases over clauses. The economy grew rapidly → The rapid growth of the economy. The government decided to invest in schools → The government's decision to invest in schools. This is called nominalization.

Nominalization compresses information and lets you build a long noun phrase as the subject of a complex sentence: The rapid growth of urban populations in developing countries has placed considerable pressure on housing markets. One sentence; densely packed; Band 7.5+.

Take 10 verb-driven sentences and convert each into a sentence built around a noun phrase. Common nominalizations: increase / rise / decline / development / introduction / implementation / availability / awareness / contribution.

Over-nominalization makes prose dense and impersonal. Mix nominalized sentences with verb-driven ones. Also keep article + preposition correct: the introduction of, the development of, the increase in (NOT the increase of).

days.deliverable: 10 verb-driven → noun-phrase rewrites + 5 collocations memorised

days.title: Articles — Advanced & Tricky Cases

blocks.content: Beyond a/an/the basics, Band 7+ requires control of: zero article with abstract / uncountable / general-plural nouns (education is important, NOT the education); generic the for species/instruments/inventions (the elephant is endangered, the piano, the internet); article + abstract noun when the noun is specified (the education of girls in rural areas).

Society (zero) vs the society of ancient Egypt (specified). Life (zero) vs the life of a refugee. Man meaning humanity (zero) vs the man (specific). Geographic names: the United States, the UK, the Sahara, the Atlantic but India, France, Mount Everest, Lake Victoria.

Write 12 sentences on academic topics (education, technology, the environment, government, family, society) carefully choosing zero / a / the. Then check each — most candidates over-use the with abstract nouns.
❌ The education is the key to success. ✔ Education is the key to success. ❌ The Mount Everest. ✔ Mount Everest.

days.deliverable: 12 article-controlled sentences + a personal list of 10 abstract nouns you keep over-articling

days.title: Week 5 Review — rewrite a paragraph using nominalization + hedging

blocks.content: Source: People use a lot of social media. Social media affects mental health. Young people are addicted. The government should ban it.

Rewrite as a 100-word academic paragraph using: at least 2 nominalizations, 3 hedges, 2 advanced linkers (one cause/effect, one concession), and 1 reference device (this trend / such concerns / the former). Aim for 3–4 sentences.
Mark each rewrite tool with a different colour. If your paragraph still reads like a list, the issue is sentence variety, not vocabulary. Apply at least 1 complex sentence (3+ clauses) from Week 3.

days.deliverable: 100-word academic rewrite with all 5 cohesion-and-hedging tools annotated

weeks.title: Week 6 — Lexico-Grammar & IELTS Polish
weeks.goal: Final week. Lock down the lexico-grammar details examiners notice last but mark first: dependent prepositions, collocations, word formation, and punctuation. Then put everything into a full Task 2 essay with a grammar checklist.

days.title: Dependent Prepositions

blocks.content: Many adjectives, verbs, and nouns require a specific preposition. Adjectives: interested in, capable of, responsible for, dependent on, aware of, similar to, different from, suitable for, accustomed to. Verbs: depend on, refer to, consist of, result in, contribute to, comply with, agree with/on/to, succeed in. Nouns: increase in, decrease in, reason for, cause of, effect on, demand for, solution to.

Wrong dependent preposition is the most common lexical-grammar error in Band 6 writing. Different from, not different than in formal British English. Solution to, not solution of. Effect on, not effect to.
Make a personal preposition cheat-sheet: 20 collocations grouped by part of speech (adjective + prep, verb + prep, noun + prep). Test yourself: cover the prepositions; supply them; check.
❌ It depends of the situation. ✔ It depends on the situation. ❌ The reason of the problem. ✔ The reason for the problem.

days.deliverable: Personal cheat-sheet of 20 dependent-preposition collocations, self-tested

days.title: Collocations & Lexical Chunks

blocks.content: Collocations = words that habitually go together. Strong tea not powerful tea. Take a decision not do a decision. Pose a threat, raise concerns, play a role, achieve a goal, meet a deadline, make progress, conduct research, tackle a problem.

Band 7 says uses less common lexical items with awareness of style. Collocations are exactly that. Single Band 7+ verbs (conduct, tackle, pose, devise, foster, exacerbate) cluster with specific nouns — using them correctly signals upper-band lexis.

Memorise 25 IELTS-relevant collocations, grouped by topic: Education (acquire knowledge, develop skills, sit an exam, drop out), Environment (mitigate climate change, reduce emissions, deplete resources), Health (lead a sedentary lifestyle, prevent diseases, raise awareness), Technology (rely heavily on, foster innovation, embrace digital tools).
Open your latest Task 2 essay. Find 5 plain phrases (think about, talk about, do something about) and replace with collocations (address the issue, discuss the matter, take action on).

days.deliverable: 25 topic-grouped collocations memorised + 5 phrase upgrades in your essay

days.title: Word Formation — Affixation

blocks.content: Move flexibly across word families using prefixes and suffixes. produce → product, productivity, productive, unproductive, productively, reproduce. govern → government, governance, governable, ungovernable. economy → economic, economical, economist, economically, uneconomic.
Band 7+ writers don't repeat the same form. They turn a verb into a noun, an adjective, an adverb as needed. The decision was wise → The wisdom of the decision → It was decided wisely.

Pick 15 high-frequency IELTS topic words (educate, develop, sustain, govern, employ, environment, technology, society, economy, urban, industry, culture, science, communicate, pollute). For each, write the noun, verb, adjective, adverb — even if some don't exist, knowing this is part of word-form control.
economic (related to the economy) vs economical (saving money) — different meanings, both correct words. historic (significant in history) vs historical (related to history). Examiners notice these.

days.deliverable: 15-word family table (noun/verb/adjective/adverb) + economic-vs-economical type pairs

days.title: Advanced Punctuation — semicolons, colons, dashes

blocks.content: Joins two related independent clauses without a conjunction: Many students struggle with grammar; few are taught it explicitly. Also separates items in a complex list where items contain commas. Use sparingly — one or two per essay max.

Introduces a list, an explanation, or a quotation after an independent clause: There are three causes: poor diet, lack of exercise, and stress. Don't use a colon after a verb or preposition (❌ The causes are: poor diet…).

Em-dash — used in pairs to embed extra info, like commas but stronger: Most countries — including developed nations — face this problem. Or singly to add an afterthought. Optional, but adds variety. Don't use more than once per paragraph.

Write 6 sentences: 2 with semicolons (joining clauses), 2 with colons (introducing a list/explanation), 2 with em-dashes (embedded info). Then take a recent essay paragraph and add ONE semicolon and ONE colon where appropriate without breaking flow.

days.deliverable: 6 advanced-punctuation sentences + 1 paragraph upgraded with one semicolon and one colon

days.title: Top 20 Band 7+ Grammar Errors to Avoid

blocks.content: (1) Different than → different from. (2) Despite + clause → use despite the fact that. (3) Will in if-clause → never in standard conditionals. (4) Most of people → most people. (5) Article omitted with abstract noun (the education) when none needed. (6) Subject-verb disagreement with each / every / one of (Each of the students has, not have). (7) Maybe as the only speculation tool. (8) Comma splice — joining two clauses with only a comma. (9) Dangling participle (Walking down the street, the rain started). (10) Although…but in the same sentence.

(11) Information / advice / research / equipment as plurals — they're uncountable. (12) Repeating the same linker (Firstly… Secondly… Thirdly…). (13) Using nowadays in every paragraph. (14) Confusing economic / economical, historic / historical. (15) People is instead of people are. (16) Discuss about → discuss takes no preposition. (17) In nowadays → nowadays alone. (18) Mixed tenses without justification. (19) I am agree → I agree. (20) Over-using very — replace with strong adjectives (very big → huge, very important → crucial).
Take a Task 2 essay you've written. Audit every clause against these 20 errors. Mark every issue. Rewrite the worst-affected paragraph from scratch.

days.deliverable: Self-audit of one full Task 2 essay against the 20-error checklist

days.title: Full Writing Task 2 with Grammar Checklist

blocks.content: Write a full Writing Task 2 essay (250–280 words) under exam conditions (40 minutes) on: Many people believe that international tourism brings more harm than good. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

After writing, audit your essay against this checklist: ✓ at least 8 different tenses ✓ at least 2 conditionals (one Second + one Third or Mixed or Inverted) ✓ at least 1 cleft sentence OR inversion ✓ at least 2 relative clauses (1 defining + 1 non-defining with commas) ✓ at least 1 participle clause ✓ at least 1 perfect modal ✓ at least 2 hedging tools ✓ at least 1 nominalization ✓ at least 1 advanced linker each for contrast / cause / concession ✓ correct articles on abstract nouns ✓ no item from the Day 40 error list.

Tick each item. Where missing, rewrite ONE sentence to include it — without forcing it. The final essay should still read naturally. Time the audit (15 minutes max) — this is exactly what you'll do in the exam's last few minutes.

days.deliverable: 250+ word Task 2 essay with all 11 checklist items ticked and annotated

days.title: Self-Assessment & Personalized Practice Plan

blocks.content: Score your Day 41 essay against the official Band Descriptors (search IELTS Writing Task 2 band descriptors). Be honest. For each of the 4 criteria — Task Response, Coherence & Cohesion, Lexical Resource, Grammatical Range & Accuracy — write your estimated band and ONE specific reason.
From the 6 weeks: which day's topic still feels shaky? Did you struggle most with conditionals, complex sentences, articles, or hedging? List your top 3 weak areas.

Build a follow-up 30-day plan: Days 1–10 = revisit your top 3 weak topics with fresh examples. Days 11–20 = write one Task 2 essay every other day, applying weak-area focus. Days 21–30 = full mock tests under timed conditions, with self-audit using the Day 41 checklist.

You've put in ~42 hours of focused grammar practice. Compare your Day 1 baseline writing with your Day 41 essay. Notice the structural variety, the conditional control, the cohesion. That's the difference between Band 6.5 and Band 7+ — and it's now in your toolkit.

days.deliverable: Self-assessed Task 2 with band estimate per criterion + personalized 30-day follow-up plan
